coredata.dc:title: Differentially expressed profiles of long non-coding RNA in responses to graphene quantum dots in microglia through analysis of microarray data
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Tianshu
Liang, Xue
Li, Yimeng
Liu, Xi
Tang, Meng
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a bright prospect to be applied in the field of biomedicine and have been reported to be associated with adverse events in the central nervous system, but it is still not clear of their underlying mechanisms. After observing the adverse effects, including decreased cell viability, DNA damage, and impairments of intracellular structure, caused by nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and amino-functionalized GQDs (A-GQDs), a microarray analysis was conducted to comprehensively gain the information on the neurotoxic molecular mechanisms of N-GQDs and A-GQDs, which is associated with long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) proposed to participate into multiple neuronal disorders. The exclusive differentially expressed lncRNA (DElncRNA) transcripts by N-GQDs at different exposure doses or A-GQDs were collected to do the functional analysis in order to find the pathways of GQDs causing toxic effects in microglia under different circumstances. Meanwhile, the findings suggested the pathways in which the shared DElncRNAs involved could play indispensable roles in neurotoxicity of N-GQDs and GQDs no matter with what surface modification, such as wnt, calcium and MAPK signalling pathways. Moreover, protein-coding genes cis- and/or trans-regulated by these DElncRNAs or transcriptional factors (TFs) interacted with DElncRNAs were detected, like sox4, hoxc9 and pou3f1, in this study. Therefore, lncRNAs identified by the omic approach could be useful candidate biomarkers or protective targets in neurotoxicity of GQDs, which indicated the pivotal role of post-transcriptional regulation in toxicity of graphene-based nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Long non-coding RNA
BV2 cells
Transcriptional factor
Surface modification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The characterization of N-GQDs and A-GQDs
Intracellular distribution of GQDs in cells
RNA extraction and lncRNA microarray assay
Analysis of differentially expressed RNA express
Quantitative real-time reverse-transcription polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) analysis
The internalization of N-GQDs and A-GQDs caused DNA damage in BV2 cells
DElncRNAs caused by N-GQDs and A-GQDs in BV2 cells
The verification of lncRNAs and the top 1 KEGG pathway

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The genotoxicity of GQDs in microglia. (A) The effects of 2.5 to 500 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs treatments for 24 h in the cell viability. (B) Representative fluorescent images showing the internalization of 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs in BV2 cells treated with GQDs for 24 h. The nucleuses were identified by OA (green), and GQDs were showing blue. (C) Representative fluorescent images of nucleus of BV2 cells treated with 100 μg/mL N-GQDs or A-GQDs for 24 h showing the DNA damages were identified by comet assay. The tail DNA percentage (D), tail length (E) and olive tail moment (F) of BV2 cells from the comet assay (n = 50). (G) TEM images showing the ultrastructure of BV2 cells treated with 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs. N indicates nucleus. NE indicates nuclear envelope. M indicates mitochondria. ER indicates endoplasmic reticulum. The green arrows indicate broken mitochondrial ridge, the blue arrows indicate collapse of mitochondrial membrane, and the yellow arrows indicate ER dilation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The heatmaps of DElncRNAs in BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL N-GQDs (A), 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (B) or 100 μg/mL A-GQDs (C) in comparison with the control; or between 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatments (n = 3). The relative expression of lncRNA is depicted based on the colour scale with −0.8 to 0.8 of fold changes. Red indicates up-regulation and blue indicates down-regulation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The number of DElncRNA in top 30 hits of GO terms annotations in 3 classifications (Biological Process, Cellular Component, Molecular Function) in BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL N-GQDs (A), 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (B) or 100 μg/mL A-GQDs (C) in comparison with the control; or between 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatments (D) (n = 3). The number of shared DElncRNA in top 30 hits of GO terms annotations in 3 classifications in BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (E) or 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs (F) in comparison with the control (n = 3). The x-axis shows the number of lncRNA and the y-axis shows the GO terms.

The number of DElncRNA in top 30 hits of KEGG pathways annotations in 5 first classifications (Cellular Processes, Environmental Information Processing, Genetic Information Processing, Human Diseases, Metabolism, Organismal Systems) in BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL N-GQDs (A), 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (B) or 100 μg/mL A-GQDs (C) in comparison with the control; or between 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatments (D) (n = 3). The number of shared DElncRNA in top 30 hits of KEGG pathways annotations in 5 first classifications in BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (E) or 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs (F) in comparison with the control (n = 3). The x-axis shows the number of lncRNA and the y-axis shows the KEGG pathways.

Top 20 cis-regulation between lncRNAs and genes in BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL N-GQDs (A), 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (B) or 100 μg/mL A-GQDs (C) in comparison with the control; or between 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatments (n = 3). The x-axis shows distance (negative value represents the upstream and positive value represents the downstream), and the right and left y-axis shows lncRNA and gene (mRNA), respectively. *** represents P < .001, ** represents P < .01, * represents P < .05. The same lncRNA is represented as the same colour.

The trans-interaction network of top 500 pairs of lncRNAs and genes in BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL N-GQDs (A), 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (B) or 100 μg/mL A-GQDs (C) in comparison with the control; or between 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatments (n = 3). The red circles indicate lncRNA and the green triangles indicate gene (mRNA). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The lncRNA-mRNA-TF network of top 10 of DElncRNAs in BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL N-GQDs (A), 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (B) or 100 μg/mL A-GQDs (C) in comparison with the control; or between 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatments (D) (n = 3). The lncRNA-mRNA-TF network of top 10 of shared DElncRNAs with parallel trends in BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (E) or 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs (F) in comparison with the control (n = 3). The green circles indicate lncRNA, the blue rectangles indicate mRNA, and the red arrows indicate TF. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The top 10 DElncRNAs between 25 μg/mL N-GQDs and the control in BV2 cells (n = 3).
The top 10 DElncRNAs between 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and the control in BV2 cells (n = 3).
The top 10 DElncRNAs between 100 μg/mL A-GQDs and the control in BV2 cells (n = 3).
The top 10 DElncRNAs between 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs in BV2 cells (n = 3).
qRT-PCR analysis determinations of fold changes in shared DElncRNAs in 25 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs when comparing with microarray data.
qRT-PCR analysis determinations of fold changes in shared DElncRNAs in 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs when comparing with microarray data.

head.ce:title: Differentially expressed profiles of long non-coding RNA in responses to graphene quantum dots in microglia through analysis of microarray data

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education; School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, P.R.China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medicine, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medicine, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, P.R.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a bright prospect to be applied in the field of biomedicine and have been reported to be associated with adverse events in the central nervous system, but it is still not clear of their underlying mechanisms. After observing the adverse effects, including decreased cell viability, DNA damage, and impairments of intracellular structure, caused by nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and amino-functionalized GQDs (A-GQDs), a microarray analysis was conducted to comprehensively gain the information on the neurotoxic molecular mechanisms of N-GQDs and A-GQDs, which is associated with long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) proposed to participate into multiple neuronal disorders. The exclusive differentially expressed lncRNA (DElncRNA) transcripts by N-GQDs at different exposure doses or A-GQDs were collected to do the functional analysis in order to find the pathways of GQDs causing toxic effects in microglia under different circumstances. Meanwhile, the findings suggested the pathways in which the shared DElncRNAs involved could play indispensable roles in neurotoxicity of N-GQDs and GQDs no matter with what surface modification, such as wnt, calcium and MAPK signalling pathways. Moreover, protein-coding genes cis- and/or trans-regulated by these DElncRNAs or transcriptional factors (TFs) interacted with DElncRNAs were detected, like sox4, hoxc9 and pou3f1, in this study. Therefore, lncRNAs identified by the omic approach could be useful candidate biomarkers or protective targets in neurotoxicity of GQDs, which indicated the pivotal role of post-transcriptional regulation in toxicity of graphene-based nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: A novel kind of quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials, called graphene quantum dots (GQDs), consist of single or multiple layers of grapheme and are below 10 nm of size (Schroeder et al., 2016). Apart from some structural attributes inherited from graphene, GQDs present excellent characteristics of quantum dots (QDs), including desirable photostability, powerful photoluminescence (PL) and good biocompatibility, which makes them become promising fluorescent probes in bio-imaging and bio-sensing (Ritter and Lyding, 2009; Li et al., 2013). Researchers have found that GQDs could be a future medicine and scope of drugless treatment in the diagnosis and therapeutics of brain tumours and neurodegenerative disorders (Henna and Pramod, 2020; Su et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2018). Nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) is a novel type of GQDs with multiple advantages in optoelectronic and biomedical applications because the nitrogen dopants is helpful in not only maintaining the intrinsic properties of GQDs, but also performing the enhanced photochemical and electrochemical activities (Schroeder et al., 2016; Kaur et al., 2018; Kuo et al., 2018). The increasing applications of N-GQDs illustrated the importance of their bio-safety assessment.

So far, there has not yet a consistent conclusion on the toxicity of N-GQDs. On the one hand, some researchers suggested the low cytotoxicity of N-GQDs (Xuan et al., 2018). On the other hand, some researchers demonstrated that cellular internalized N-GQDs would damage normal activity of some critical enzymes (Deng et al., 2019). According to available studies, GQDs highly contributed to the diagnosis, prevention and treatment of brain diseases because their small size enables GQDs easy to translocate and accumulated in the CNS by passing the blood-brain barrier (Henna and Pramod, 2020; Kim et al., 2018; Xiao et al., 2016), which makes the central nervous system (CNS) be one of target organs of GQDs (Schroeder et al., 2016) (Facciola et al., 2019). Therefore, it is probability of GQDs causing CNS damages. Microglia are residential immunity cells in the CNS and play a critical role in the development and homeostasis of brain. Evaluating the harmful effects of nanoparticles in microglia is an important part of risk assessment of nanoparticles in the CNS. The microglial injuries caused by nanomaterials, especially carbon-based nanoparticles, could result in serious brain disorders (Li and Barres, 2018; Bussy et al., 2015). Unfortunately, the toxicity study of GQDs on microglia is still rare.

As we known, the information on concrete regulatory mechanisms of N-GQDs changing the expression of genes in cells could be a critical foundation of study on toxicity of N-GQDs. Right now, a number of studies have reported the involvement of long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) in the regulation of gene transcription, post-transcription and epigenetic modulation, which can explain the molecular mechanisms of various nanomaterials inducing toxicity (Pan et al., 2019; Kong et al., 2019). Owning to an lncRNA microarray approach, the abnormal expressions of certain lncRNAs can be identified in the toxicity of N-GQDs on microglia, which provides valuable data in the risk assessment of GQDs before their massive biomedical applications.

Even now the number of studies focusing on the lncRNA expression levels altered by carbon-based nanoparticles is rare, but the importance of lncRNAs in regulating cellular responses to carbon-based nanoparticles has been agreed by most researchers (Wu et al., 2016a; Das et al., 2015). Hence, the aim of this study is to explore the function of lncRNAs altered by GQD exposure in microglia, which is the first time to link GQDs exposure to neurotoxic consequences at the lncRNA level. Firstly, after identifying the differentially expressed lncRNAs (DElncRNAs) caused by GQDs in microglial BV2 cells, potential Gene Ontology (GO) terms and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathways significantly annotated by DElncRNAs were incorporated, and the crosstalk of lncRNA, mRNA and transcription factor (TF) was analysed as well. Some of the differentially expressed genes and functions identified by the bioinformation analysis were validated by PCR technique and cellular assays.

Furthermore, since the surface functionalization has been reported to affect the regulations of genes in some toxicity studies on carbon-based nanoparticles (Wu et al., 2016a; Fröhlich et al., 2014), the effects of amino-functionalized GQDs (A-GQDs) on the gene expression was assessed in this study in order to identify the similarity and distinction of the toxicity of GQDs with different surface modifications. To sum up, this study is trying to broaden the knowledge about neurotoxicity of GQDs and associated signalling pathways, eventually providing data of molecular mechanisms for deep risk assessment of GQDs and grapheme-based nanomaterials.
Cell Counting Kit 8 and propidium iodide (PI) dye were purchased from Yeasen Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Acridine orange (AO) dye was purchased from Solarbio, (Beijing, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of N-GQDs and A-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs and A-GQDs used in this study were purchased from XFNANO materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and sizes of them were presented in Fig. S1. Specific data of physicochemical properties of both GQDs were evaluated and showed in the supporting information Table S1.

The microglial BV2 cell line was obtained from the Shanghai Cell Research Centre (Shanghai, China). The cell maintained medium was Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) high glucose medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 100 U/mL Penicillin and changed every 1– 2 days. The cell cultured condition was a humidified atmosphere of 95% O2 and 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

Cell viability was measured using the Cell Counting Kit 8 (CCK8) according to the manufacturer's protocol. The administrative concentrations of N-GQDs and A-GQDs both were 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 250 and 500 μg/mL, and the administrative time was 24 h. The CCK8 assay was performed independently in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular distribution of GQDs in cells
ce:section.ce:para: The internalization of N-GQDs and A-GQDs were determined by a confocal microscope with acridine orange (AO) marking nuclei in BV2 cells treated with 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs for 24 h.

The DNA damage of BV2 cells treated with 25 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs or A-GQDs were measured by the comet assay, which was carried out as described as before (He et al., 2019). The tail DNA percentage, tail length and Olive tail moment of 50 cells that were randomly selected in each group were analysed by using the Comet Assay Software Project (V 1.2.3b1).

The BV2 cells were fixed in PBS containing 2.5% glutaradehyde, followed by the standard fixation protocol described in a previous study (Wu et al., 2016b). Finally, the ultrathin sections of cells were observed by a TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA extraction and lncRNA microarray assay
ce:section.ce:para: The Trizol reagent was used to extract total RNAs following with the manufacturer's protocol. The quantity and quality of RNAs were measured at A260/A280 nm using a NanoDrop ND-2000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific) and an Agilent Bioanalyzer 2100 (Agilent Technologies), respectively. Samples with RNA Integrity Number (RIN) over seven proceeds can be used for lncRNA microarray detection. The Agilent mouse RNA microarrays were implemented based on several authoritative databases. The process of microarray assay was completed by OE Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of differentially expressed RNA express
ce:section.ce:para: The feature extraction (version 10.7.11, Agilent Technologies) was used to obtain raw data that were analysed using Genespring (version 13.1, Agilent Technologies). The standards of fold change (FC) ≥ 1.5 and p value ≤.05 were set to detect significantly different mRNA or lncRNA. GO and KEGG analysis were applied to determine the roles of differentially expressed mRNA and correspondingly potential co-expressed mRNAs of differentially expressed lncRNA. Afterword, the different patterns of gene expression were showed by performing hierarchical clustering.
The pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) >0.8 with a false discovery rate (FDR)-adjusted p value ≤.05 are set to find co-expressed lncRNA and mRNA.

The function of DElncRNAs is predicted depending on the functional annotations of corresponding co-expressed mRNAs. Both p value <.05 and FDR <0.05 were set to analysing enriched GO terms and KEGG pathways.

The cis-analysis tries to find cis-regulated gene that is protein-coding gene being within 100 kb in genomic distance from a DElncRNA on the same allele and co-expressed with the corresponding lncRNA. These genes are close to the genome and co-expressed in the expression pattern, which are likely to be regulated by lncRNAs.

According to the differentially expressed genes, lncRNAs and mRNAs that are not in the same chromosome are selected as candidate targets. The RNA interaction software RIsearch-2.0 was used to predict the trans-regulation between lncRNA and mRNA based on the screening condition that are the base number of direct interaction between two nucleic acid molecules is more than 10, and the base binding free energy is less than - 30.
The mRNAs co-expressed with the DElncRNAs were identified, and then the TFs that regulate these mRNAs were recognized. The ternary relation network of lncRNA-mRNA-TF was drawn using R package.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative real-time reverse-transcription polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Equal quantities of total RNA of BV2 cells were used to do qRT-PCR analysis. The qRT-PCR primers were designed by software Primer Premier based on National Centre for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) (Table S2). The relative quantities of lncRNA were normalized against the internal reference gene Glyceraldehyde-3-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (gapdh). Three replicates were conducted for each qRT-PCR analysis.

All data were displayed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was performed using GraphePad Prism 6.0 (GraphPad Software, CA, USA). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to determine the statistical significance between control and exposed groups, followed by the Dunnett's t-test to determine the significance of differences between groups. Probability levels of <0.05, <0.01 and <0.001 were considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The internalization of N-GQDs and A-GQDs caused DNA damage in BV2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: When BV2 cells were exposed to N-GQDs or A-GQDs for 24 h, the cell viability decreased following with the increasing exposure concentrations, and the cell viability decreased significantly compared to the control in BV2 cells exposed to GQDs at concentrations over 50 μg/mL (Fig. 1A). According to the representative images, the fluorescence of N-GQDs and A-GQDs was observed in the whole BV2 cells, especially in the nucleus (Fig. 1B). After that, the comet assay was used to detect DNA damages caused by GQDs. The results showed that those indicators, i.e. the tail DNA percentage, tail length and olive tail moment, in BV2 cells exposed to 100 μg/mL N-GQD and A-GQDs were significantly different from the control, while 25 μg/mL GQDs with two surface modifications did not cause any DNA damage (Fig. 1C–F).

Moreover, the intracellular structure of BV2 cells showed by TEM images suggested that the nucleus morphology obviously altered after 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs exposure, apart from the impairments of mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum (ER), including broken mitochondrial ridge, collapse of mitochondrial membrane and ER dilation (Fig. 1G). Based on the injuries in cell viability and DNA, N-GQDs commonly caused slightly severer toxic effects than A-GQDs in BV2 cells when the exposure concentration and time were the same.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DElncRNAs caused by N-GQDs and A-GQDs in BV2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: There are 38, 348 and 411 differentially expressed lncRNAs (DElncRNAs) changing from 25 μg/mL N-GQDs, 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatment groups when compared to the control, while only 12 DElncRNAs were identified between 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatment groups (Table S3). Among them, the numbers of up-regulated and down-regulated DElncRNAs in each group were showed in Table S3 and Fig. S2. Intriguingly, there are only 25 DElncRNAs that were on a consistent trend in the two N-GQDs treatment groups, while 207 DElncRNAs in the N-GQDs treatment group were consistent with those in the A-GQDs treatment group when the administration dose was same (Fig. S3).

The predicted function of lncRNAs depends on their co-expressed mRNAs, and the co-expressed mRNAs of corresponding top 10 DElncRNAs in each group were showed in Table S4 to S7. Since a certain lncRNA can take part into different GO categories and KEGG pathways, top 100 annotations were counted to predict primary functions and signalling pathways that were regulated by DElncRNAs. For the GO term annotations, three classifications of DElncRNAs, i.e. biological process, cellular component and molecular function, were analysed separately. In the 25 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment group, most DElncRNAs were enriched in the terms of cell body fiber, centrosome, transcription factor activity, and RNA polymerase II transcription factor recruiting (Fig. 3A). In the N-GQDs and A-GQDs both at 100 μg/mL treatment groups, DElncRNAs were mainly clustered in terms of retinol binding and DNA binding, respectively (Fig. 3B and C). When analysing DElncRNAs between N-GQDs and A-GQDs treatment groups, they were chiefly enriched into defense response to virus (Fig. 3D). The shared DElncRNAs in BV2 cells treated with N-GQDs at different concentrations mainly enriched into transcription factor TFIIA complex, core promoter binding, RNA polymerase II proximal promoter sequence-specific DNA binding (Fig. 3E), while the lncRNAs that both were dysregulated by GQDs with two surface modifications chiefly clustered in terms of transcription factor TFIIA complex, inner dynein arm, NSL complex, integral component of organelle membrane, MLL3/4 complex, oxidoreductase activity, acting on CH-OH group of donors, and flavin adenine dinucleotide binding (Fig. 3F).

For the KEGG pathway analysis, many pathways were involved in the toxicity of N-GQDs, such as wnt signalling pathway, calcium signalling pathway and mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signalling pathway, or the toxicity of A-GQDs, such as serotonergic synapse and signalling pathways regulating pluripotency of stem cells (Fig. 4A~C). When analysing the DElncRNAs between N-GQDs and A-GQDs treatments, most lncRNAs were clustered into ether lipid metabolism (Fig. 4D).

Simultaneously, shared DElncRNAs in treatments of N-GQDs with two concentrations and GQDs with two surface modifications were subjected to KEGG pathway analysis as well by counting top 100 annotations according to the p-values rank, and most frequently predicted KEGG pathways were ecm-receptor-interaction (ECM)-receptor interaction and glycolsaminoglycan biosynthesis, respectively (Fig. 4E and F). When analysing the top 1 significantly shared DElncRNA in each group (Table S8 and S9), the GO terms and KEGG pathways that were enriched by its co-expressed mRNAs were showed in Fig. S3 and S4.

The cis-regulated mRNAs co-expressed with lncRNAs were identified by screening the correlation coefficient is above 0.9. Fig. 5A–C showed top 20 cis-regulated genes and their corresponded DElncRNAs caused by exposure of 50 μg/mL N-GQDs, 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, while only one lncRNA transcript having the function of cis-regulation was found different between two types of GQDs treatments (Fig. 5D). The trans-interaction between protein-coding genes and their corresponding lncRNAs in each group were showed in Fig. 6 .

The co-expressed mRNAs of top 10 DElncRNAs in each group was used to recognize their regulated TFs, and the network of them were presented in Fig. 7 . According to the network, most DElncRNAs caused by the 25 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment participating in the pathways were regulated by TFs of Homeobox C9 (hoxc9) and POU Class 3 Homeobox 1 (pou3f1) (Fig. 7A). In the treatment groups of 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs, hoxc9 was the main TF in which lncRNAs both were predicted to participate (Fig. 7B and C). TFs of MAF BZIP Transcription (mafb), Factor B Interferon Regulatory Factor 2 (irf2) and CCAAT Enhancer Binding Protein Alpha (cebpa) were involved in the participation of DElncRNAs between two types of GQDs treatments (Fig. 7D). In this study, the DElncRNAs that were both caused by the treatments of N-GQDs with two concentrations were used to predict their TFs, and intriguingly, hoxc9 and pou3f1 were found here as well (Fig. 7E). The mainly predicted TFs regulated by shared DElncRNAs with parallel trends in treatment groups of two types of GQDs included Spi-B Transcription Factor (spib), Eomesodermin (eomes), Activating Transcription Factor 3 (atf3) and Special AT-Rich Sequence Binding Protein 1 (satb1), etc. (Fig. 7F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The verification of lncRNAs and the top 1 KEGG pathway
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, separated 4 shared DElncRNAs transcripts in two N-GQDs treatments groups or in two types of GQDs treatment groups were chosen to validate the results from microarray through RT-qPCR analysis. In order to corroborate the cis- and trans-regulating potentials of lncRNAs, Gm26512 and LOC102636183 were chosen. And residues were randomly selected from top 10 shared DElncRNAs altered by both 25 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs, or 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, separately (Table S8 and S9). As shown in Tables 5 and 6
, the FC in expressions of all selected lncRNAs was at the same trends in the corresponding treatment groups. The consistence between data from PCR and microarray analysis confirmed the reliability of microarray data.

Following with the increasing applications of GQDs in widely fields, the exposure nanoscale materials to the public and the environments is rising. Thus, the biosafety of GQDs attracts more and more attention. Half a decade ago, researchers have found the DNA damage in fibroblast cells caused by GQDs (Wang et al., 2015). Recently, some available studies revealed that genotoxic responses to GQDs in cultured cells and whole animals, resulted in severe consequences (Xu et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018). However, the genotoxic effects of GQDs in neural cells are not presented and the underlying molecular mechanisms are still unclear. Notably, microglia are an important type of neural cells in the CNS and involved in the normal function of brain. In this study, the damages caused by two types of GQDs in the aspect of genotoxicity in microglial BV2 cells were assessed to provide value data on the biosafety of GQDs in the CNS.

Firstly, N-GQDs and A-GQDs were both observed to be taken up inside of BV2 cells and distributed into the whole cell. Apart from reduction in the cell viability induced by the GQDs treatments, the DNA damages caused by GQDs, including disruption of nucleic acid and altered morphology of nuclei, were detected as well. As a result, the effects of GQDs on the expression profile of lncRNA were investigated due to the significance of epigenetics in GQDs' toxicity. Since 25 μg/mL N-GQDs did not cause obviously cytotoxic and genotoxic effects in BV2 cells but the cytotoxicity and genotoxicity of 100 μg/mL N-GQDs were obvious, these two concentrations were selected for the microarray to obtain sensitive non-coding RNAs under the circumstances of non- or slight cytotoxicity of N-GQDs. 100 μg/mL A-GQDs were chosen to compare the 100 μg/mL N-GQDs because A-GQDs exposure commonly caused milder toxic effects than N-GQDs when the administration dose and time were same.

According to the results of microarray, there are a number of lncRNAs altered differentially by treatments of 25 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs when compared with the control in BV2 cells. The functional assessments were performed based on the co-expressed protein-coding mRNAs of DElncRNAs. The findings suggested that wnt signalling pathway was the overlapping KEGG pathway in which DElncRNAs caused by the treatments of N-GQDs at two concentrations were enriched, which indicated that the wnt signalling pathways involved in the cellular adverse effects caused by N-GQDs exposure with the non-cytotoxicity dose increasing to slight cytotoxicity dose. More importantly, the shared DElncRNAs with the same trend in two N-GQDs treatment groups were clustered into KEGG signalling pathways, such as ECM-receptor interaction, which also could be play indispensable roles in N-GQDs causing cytotoxicity in microglia. The N-GQDs-caused dysregulation of these signalling pathways was capable of damaging the normal cellular process.

Meanwhile, the KEGG pathways enriched by exclusive DElncRNAs caused by 25 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment were also important because they could take part in the early biological process induced by N-GQDs without remarkable cytotoxicity and genotoxicity. On the contrary, the lncRNAs exclusively altered by 100 μg/mL N-GQDs exposure could be not sensitive to non-cytotoxic exposure dose of N-GQDs but to a higher one, which provided a hint that these DElncRNAs play key roles in the regulation of N-GQDs causing microglial cytotoxicity. Moreover, these DElncRNAs being stable under the low-dose circumstance could probably participate in the late stage of biological process in cells.

Of note, the DElncRNAs could participate in the toxicity effects induced by GQDs in microglia via cis- or trans-regulating corresponding mRNAs. In two N-GQDs treatment groups, DElncRNAs mainly cis-regulated mRNAs of Parvin Alpha (parva) and pou3f3. The former one involves in the establishment of a series of cell processes, such as cell polarity, cell adhesion, cell spreading and cell migration, and the latter acted synergistically with SRY (Sex Determining Region Y)-Box 4 (sox4) and involves in neuronal development. Meanwhile, Sodium/Potassium Transporting ATPase Interacting 3 (nkain3) encoding a cation transporter, and Leucine Rich Repeat Containing 61 (lrrc61), a hypermethylated gene, were mostly trans-regulated by lncRNAs changed differentially by 25 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs, respectively. The findings indicated that N-GQDs could damage the critical biological functioning and signalling events in microglia through mRNAs cis- or trans-regulated by DElncRNAs.

Additionally, lncRNAs could regulate a certain protein-coding gene via the interaction with certain TFs and resulted in unfortunate cell density. In this study, hoxc9 and pou3f1 were TFs that chiefly regulated the mRNAs co-expressed of DElncRNAs caused by N-GQDs. Researchers have found that hoxc9 could be involved in neuronal cell proliferation and differentiation (Yang et al., 2019). As we predicted, lncRNAs involved in hoxc9-mediated pathways probably could be an explanation of the reduction in cell viability caused by N-GQDs exposure. Pou3f1, also known as oct6, is a TF acting important roles in many neuronal genes that link to neurogenesis (Ilia, 2004). Since pou3f1 is stably expressed in the mammalian CNS, the dysregulation of it could take part in microglial damages caused by N-GQDs exposure.

In this study, both N-GQDs and A-GQDs at the exposure dose 100 μg/mL could decrease the cell viability and damage DNA and organelles in microglia, but the adverse effects elicited by A-GQDs were weaker than N-GQDs. However, the potential molecular mechanisms of GQDs causing these different phenotypes were unclear. Therefore, 100 μg/mL A-GQDs were chosen in order to assess the difference and similarity of toxicity GQDs with different surface modifications in microglia. It is intriguing that DElncRNAs in BV2 cells between N-GQDs and A-GQDs were mainly enriched in a few GO terms that were related with the nuclei, like centrosome and DNA binding. The findings could be explained by the fact of amino functionalized group is capable of promoting the linkage between materials and organic molecules (Mishra and Bhat, 2019; Sahiner et al., 2019; Junkermeier et al., 2013). The ether lipid metabolism is a KEGG signalling pathway mainly enriched by DElncRNAs between N-GQDs and A-GQDs. The rich in unsaturated fatty acids makes brain susceptible to dysregulation of lipid metabolism (Migliore et al., 2015). Ether lipid metabolism has been found to play an important role in mediating neurotransmission through altering the properties of synaptic vesicles (Dorninger et al., 2019).

In the present, although there is not enough data to draw a conclusion in the effects of certain physicochemical parameters of GQDs on the physiological interactions of cells, the researching experience and findings could provide valuable references for further study. In this study, the findings suggested that different surface modifications could cause adverse effects in microglia via different toxic pathways regulated by lncRNAs. Apart from treatments of GQDs with different surface modifications might resulting in different neuronal disorders, surface charge associated with surface chemical modifications could influence the physiology of cells, such as uptake rates and efficiency of nanoparticles (Cheng et al., 2013).

In the meantime, we found mafb and cebpa are the main TFs that were predicted by the regulation of protein-coding mRNAs of corresponding lncRNAs differentially expressed between two types of GQDs. Mafb is a member of the large Maf family of TFs and a critical regulation of cell differentiation in many cells. Researchers have found the crucial role of mafb in microglia for development of pathogenesis in animal models (Tozaki-Saitoh et al., 2019). Cebpa, also known as C/EBP-α, is a TF that coordinates proliferation arrest and has been found as a regulatory molecule involved in the differentiation and activation of microglia (Ponomarev et al., 2011). Since these two TFs play critical roles in the normal function of microglia, their dysregulations could be closely related to the differently adverse effects caused by N-GQDs or A-GQDs in microglia. Similar to many studies suggesting the influences of surface modification in the biocompatibility, internalization and cytotoxicity of nanomaterials, these sensitive biomarkers, i.e. lncRNAs, identified herein could be attributed to the surface functionalization of GQDs.

However, we also try to find the similar mechanistic pathways of GQDs with different surface modifications causing damages in microglia through analysing the DElncRNAs that were both induced by N-GQDs and A-GQDs exposure. There were many overlapping KEGG signalling pathways and TFs that could be more likely indispensably contributing to GQDs-induced toxic effects in the cellular processes of microglia, including cell proliferation, cell cycle regulation, cell differentiation and inflammatory responses, etc. The ultimate aim of assessing the toxicity of GQDs is to find low-toxicity but good-efficient GQDs in wide applications. Identification of sensitive biomarkers influenced by GQDs through microarray analysis could be a first step to achieve the best balance point. However, this study have not reported the specific regulation or interaction of lncRNAs with signalling pathways and TFs, which needs to be required to explore in further studies.

Along with the increasing exposure of GQDs to the public and the environment, the toxicity of GQDs should be taken into account during biomedical applications. In this study, a RNA microarray approach is used to disclose the lncRNAs involved in the pathways of microglial dysfunctions in responses to GQDs with different surface modifications. These DElncRNAs diversely contributed to the neurotoxicity of GQDs through cooperating with protein-coding genes and interacting with TFs in microglia. Some pathways that play indispensable roles in GQDs with different treatment doses or surface modifications causing toxic effects were detected through analysing shared or exclusive DElncRNAs in microglia. Taken together, a series of sensitive lncRNAs identified by this high-throughput omics approach not only provide several candidate reservoirs for prevention of GQDs' toxicity through targeting lncRNAs, but also underline the guideline on further functional studies in regard to lncRNAs in the neurotoxicity of GQDs.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Tianshu Wu:Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.Xue Liang:Validation, Data curation.Yimeng Li:Validation.Xi Liu:Validation.Meng Tang:Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 21876026, 31671034, 81473003), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant number BK20180371), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (grant numbers 2242020K40192) and Supporting Program of Southeast University Zhishan Young Scholar (grant number 2242020R40140).

Table S2 Designed qRT-PCR primers of tested lncRNAs.
Table S3 The number of differently expressed lncRNA.
Table S4~S7 Top 10 pairs of lncRNA-mRNA co-expression among different groups.
Table S8 and S9 The top 10 shared lncRNAs in different groups.
Fig. S1 The TEM images and size distribution of GQDs.
Fig. S2 The volcanoplot of DElncRNAs.
Fig. S3 Venn diagram showing the number of DElncRNA.
Fig. S4 Top 10 hits of GO terms annotations.
Fig. S5 Top 10 hits of KEGG pathways annotations.
